    "R1.3": "Specific quantitative claims are made inline (pass@1 78.4% vs 71.2%; 34/80 vs 21/80 issues) without table or figure anchors visible in this section. Alignment between stated numbers and their experimental basis is reviewable and materially affects credibility.",

rationale.R1.1: The section carries a structurally unusual contribution claim: DialogueDev is introduced as the system under study, but RSP (a single-agent prompting method) is asserted as the central contribution. Assessing whether the thesis is coherent and whether the contribution framing is defensible is squarely in scope for any plausible reading of 'review'.
rationale.R1.2: Load-bearing: the paper's core argument (RSP explains most variance; multi-agent topology is secondary) depends on a logical chain that must be checked for internal consistency. Specifically, the claim that RSP gains are 'largely orthogonal' to agent-decomposition gains must follow from the evidence, and the framing must not contradict the earlier positioning of DialogueDev as a contribution.
rationale.R1.3: Specific quantitative claims are made inline (pass@1 78.4% vs 71.2%; 34/80 vs 21/80 issues) without table or figure anchors visible in this section. Alignment between stated numbers and their experimental basis is reviewable and materially affects credibility.
rationale.R1.4: RSP is presented as a novel 'chain-of-thought decomposition strategy,' but the relationship to prior CoT prompting work (e.g., self-refine, reflection-based prompting, least-to-most) is unaddressed. Assessing whether the claimed novelty delta is genuine is central to evaluating the contribution.

parsed.clarification_question: Is this a scholarly peer review focused on contribution validity, logical soundness, and evidence support — or an editorial review focused on writing quality, clarity, and narrative structure?
